title: Augmented Human Development Index
subtitle: The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living.
caption: The 2020 Augmented Human Development Index map displays national scores across a continuous color scale ranging from 0 to 0.8, where darker shades of blue represent higher levels of achievement in health, knowledge, freedom, and living standards. The highest values, falling within the 0.7 to 0.8 band, appear across western and northern Europe, including Norway, Sweden, Germany, France, and the United Kingdom, as well as in North America with Canada and the United States, and in the southern hemisphere with Australia and New Zealand. Japan and South Korea also sit in this top tier in East Asia. Intermediate scores, generally between 0.4 and 0.6, cover much of Latin America—such as Brazil, Argentina, and Chile—along with Russia, China, and parts of the Middle East like Saudi Arabia. Lower values, in the 0.2 to 0.4 range, dominate sub-Saharan Africa, including Nigeria, Ethiopia, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, as well as parts of South Asia like India and Pakistan. The lowest band, 0 to 0.2, includes countries such as Niger, Chad, and Yemen. Greenland and a few other territories are marked as having no data. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of higher index values in temperate-zone industrialized nations, while lower values cluster in tropical and conflict-affected regions. The most visible pattern is the strong regional grouping: high scores in Europe, North America, and Oceania; mid-range scores across much of Asia and Latin America; and low scores across most of Africa and parts of South Asia.
